title: Compression Necrosis Explained
excerpt: When the vessel is collapsed from outside in because the filler deposits in between the layers most of the accidents the vascular accidents we see they're compressive their stenosis like strangling the vessels through the outside and also the positives of deposits of fillers in b…

segments.text: when the vessel is collapsed from outside in because the filler deposits in between the layers most of the accidents the vascular accidents we see they're compressive their stenosis like strangling the vessels through the outside and also the positives of deposits of fillers in between the layers especially between fat and the muscular layer of the arteries there is still no occlusion that's why we see partial necrotic patches instead of full necrosis that's an interesting idea because um one of the things i've often heard from surgeons is if you tie off a vessel in just one place normally there are enough collaterals that it can supply around the other side damn i reconstruct vasculature i tie big vessels i can see people stop breathing around there is not one person because it's so scary when you

slide a vessel 90 degrees against the floor and there's no necrosis but there's no necrosis because the uh collateral anastomosis the vasculature is free to be occupied by the counterflow a cork of filler nothing moves [Music] that's when we see the difference between cutting a vessel and clogging a vessel so are you saying well if you clog if so are you saying well if you clog a vessel but it's only a short section but you're still going to get necrosis somewhere is that that's your understanding and where would you obviously is that how would you differentiate that from a very big emboli that kind of blocks what does it look like differently um those two let me see if i can pull this one is the one i'm talking about yeah you have a partial occlusion

when the vessel is collapsed from outside in because the filler deposits in between the layers you still have some blood flow this is your five seconds capillary refill against no capillary refill at all that's interesting so this is that compression now when i thought about compression i often think about larger structures but you're almost saying like it can just be right next to the artery and we know we have at least an average of four vessels that outlet internal carotid to the surface of the face around the eye socket dorsal nasal supratrochlear supraorbital and xenomatic facial which is a terminal branch it's usually not harmful even when uh compromise on the very

final segment of it these other three arteries they usually have intercommunications with other arteries either external carotid branches or collateral from the other side and still we see spread of ischemic patch at a distance and this is what explains sort of what happens when people get so shocked when they have a an ischemic patch that reaches the middle of the forehead then an injection was happening in the uh in this in the fold
